category.label: GLOSSARY
category.description: Plain-English definitions of terms you'll see on price screens, company pages, and quote tickers.

entries.title: P/E Ratio
entries.subtitle: Price-to-Earnings — how much investors pay per dollar of earnings.

actions.label: Open AAPL in Markets
actions.hint: See current P/E in the stock detail

rules_of_thumb.label: Negative EPS
rules_of_thumb.note: P/E is meaningless when earnings are negative

pitfalls: Comparing P/E across sectors without context (banks vs SaaS — useless).
Trusting forward P/E in cyclical troughs — analyst estimates lag reality.
Looking at P/E without checking debt — leverage flatters EPS.
Ignoring share buybacks that mechanically inflate EPS without growth.

entries.title: EPS
entries.subtitle: Earnings Per Share — profit attributable to each share of stock.

rules_of_thumb.label: Surprise that moves stock
rules_of_thumb.note: Beats/misses under 5% rarely move price meaningfully

pitfalls: Headline EPS beats funded by buybacks instead of revenue growth.
Forgetting diluted vs basic EPS when valuations get tight.
One-time tax benefits flowing through net income → temporary EPS lift.

entries.title: P/B Ratio
entries.subtitle: Price-to-Book — share price relative to accounting book value per share.

rules_of_thumb.label: Bank fair value
rules_of_thumb.note: P/B near 1 implies ROE ≈ cost of equity

pitfalls: Using P/B for tech / asset-light businesses — book misses intangibles.
Goodwill from acquisitions inflating book without value creation.
Treating low P/B as automatically cheap — could be a value trap.

entries.title: EV / EBITDA
entries.subtitle: Enterprise multiple — capital-structure-neutral valuation metric.
pitfalls: Trusting EBITDA without checking maintenance CapEx.
Using EV/EBITDA on banks or insurers — interest is operating, not financing.
Ignoring stock-based comp added back in 'adjusted EBITDA'.

entries.title: Free Cash Flow
entries.subtitle: Cash a business generates after reinvesting in itself.
pitfalls: Confusing operating cash flow with FCF (OCF ignores CapEx).
Using levered FCF (FCFE) for valuation that should be unlevered (FCFF).
Trusting FCF when it's funded by stretching payables.

entries.title: EBITDA
entries.subtitle: Operating performance proxy that strips out financing & accounting noise.
aliases: earnings before interest taxes depreciation amortization

rules_of_thumb.label: Adjusted EBITDA gap
rules_of_thumb.note: Add-backs may be hiding real costs

pitfalls: Treating EBITDA as 'cash flow' — it isn't, by a wide margin.
Comparing EBITDA across capital intensities (utility vs SaaS).
Ignoring stock-based comp in 'adjusted' figures.

warnings.text: EBITDA is not GAAP and can mask real cash needs (CapEx, working capital, interest). Don't use it alone.

entries.title: Beta
entries.subtitle: How much a stock moves when the broader market moves.
pitfalls: Using a 1-year beta during regime change — past beta ≠ future beta.
Confusing beta (relative) with volatility (absolute) — they're different.
Trusting beta of low-volume stocks — estimation is noisy.

entries.title: Alpha
entries.subtitle: Return above what the market would predict for your risk level.
pitfalls: Confusing single-year alpha with skill — too noisy.
Hidden factor exposure (small-cap, value) being mistaken for alpha.
Survivorship bias — failed funds don't show up in averages.

entries.title: Sharpe Ratio
entries.subtitle: Return per unit of volatility — risk-adjusted performance.

rules_of_thumb.label: Suspicious
rules_of_thumb.note: Likely backtest overfit or hidden tail risk

pitfalls: Treating upside volatility as 'risk' — Sharpe penalizes it identically.
Trusting Sharpe of strategies with fat-tailed returns (option selling).
Comparing Sharpe across non-stationary regimes.

entries.title: Drawdown
entries.subtitle: How far down you are from a previous high — the pain measure.
pitfalls: Confusing volatility with drawdown — drawdown is the felt pain.
Ignoring time-to-recover; underwater for years can be worse than depth.
Looking at backtest max drawdown without asking if you'd have held through it.

entries.title: Volatility
entries.subtitle: How much prices move around — the standard deviation of returns.
pitfalls: Using normal-distribution math for fat-tailed returns.
Mistaking realized vol for implied vol (and vice versa).
Annualizing short windows during a vol spike — overshoots.

entries.title: Implied Volatility
entries.subtitle: The volatility the option market thinks the underlying will exhibit.
pitfalls: Buying options just before earnings — vol crush often eats the directional gain.
Treating high IV as bullish/bearish — it's only directional in magnitude, not sign.
Selling far-dated IV without understanding term-structure dynamics.

warnings.text: High IV usually means premium is rich for sellers but expensive for buyers — direction-of-movement is not implied.

entries.title: Open Interest
entries.subtitle: How many derivatives contracts are still open and unsettled.

rules_of_thumb.label: Untouchable
rules_of_thumb.note: You'll get filled at terrible prices

pitfalls: Selecting strikes by 'cheap premium' without checking OI — execution slips wreck the trade.
Reading rising OI without context (price direction) — signal needs both.
Confusing volume (today's flow) with OI (alive contracts).

entries.title: Contango
entries.subtitle: When future prices are higher than spot — common in storable commodities.

rules_of_thumb.label: Long-only ETF cost
rules_of_thumb.note: Compounds over time — major USO underperformance

pitfalls: Buying long-only commodity ETFs in contango without understanding roll cost.
Ignoring storage/logistics in 'contango is normal' assumption.
Assuming contango → bullish future spot (it doesn't imply that).

entries.title: Backwardation
entries.subtitle: When future prices are below spot — typical in tight supply markets.
pitfalls: Assuming backwardation will persist — it can flip back fast.
Trading the curve shape without understanding what's driving it.

entries.title: Basis
entries.subtitle: Spot price minus the futures price for the same asset.
pitfalls: Treating basis as constant in a hedge — it isn't.
Ignoring delivery location/quality differences vs futures spec.

entries.title: Value at Risk
entries.subtitle: Largest loss you'd expect over N days at a given confidence.
pitfalls: Treating VaR as the worst case — it's the threshold, not the tail.
Trusting parametric VaR for fat-tailed returns.
Backtesting on a benign window — VaR will look great until it doesn't.

warnings.text: VaR tells you the threshold of bad days, not how bad they get past it. Pair it with CVaR or stress tests.

entries.title: Dividend Yield
entries.subtitle: Annual dividend per share divided by share price.
pitfalls: Chasing high yield without checking payout ratio + FCF coverage.
Treating yield as 'safe income' — it can be cut overnight.
Ignoring total return — low-yield growth may compound faster.

entries.title: Market Capitalization
entries.subtitle: Total dollar value of a company's outstanding shares.
pitfalls: Confusing market cap with enterprise value — leverage matters.
Ignoring float-adjusted vs full market cap (insiders / govt holdings).
Treating micro-caps like large-caps in liquidity assumptions.

entries.title: VIX
entries.subtitle: 30-day implied volatility of S&P 500 options — the market's 'fear gauge'.
pitfalls: Buying VIX after a spike — you're paying peak insurance price.
Confusing VIX (forward-looking) with realized SPX vol (historical).
Trading VIX directly — it's not investible; only futures/options/ETFs.

entries.title: Return on Equity
entries.subtitle: Net income as a percentage of shareholder equity.
pitfalls: Treating high ROE as automatic quality — leverage flatters it.
Comparing ROE across capital structures — use DuPont decomposition.
Ignoring goodwill — return on tangible equity is often more honest.

entries.title: Delta
entries.subtitle: Δ option price per $1 move in the underlying — also approx. probability of finishing ITM.
pitfalls: Treating delta as exact probability — it's an approximation, off in fat tails.
Forgetting put delta is negative; long puts hedge long stock 1-for-1 at delta ~−1.
Ignoring delta drift as gamma compounds — your hedge ratio shifts with price.

entries.title: Gamma
entries.subtitle: Rate of change of delta — the option's 'acceleration'.
pitfalls: Selling short-dated ATM options under-prices gamma — losses accelerate quickly.
Confusing gamma with vega — gamma reacts to price moves, vega to vol changes.
Gamma squeezes (e.g., GME 2021) come from dealers being short gamma at scale.

entries.title: Theta
entries.subtitle: Daily time decay — how much an option loses per day from passage of time.
pitfalls: Holding long calls 'just one more day' through theta-heavy decay.
Selling weekly premium without sizing for the gamma risk it carries.
Forgetting weekend theta — Friday close to Monday open eats 3 days of theta.

entries.title: Vega
entries.subtitle: Δ option price per 1% change in implied volatility.

rules_of_thumb.label: Vega vs theta tradeoff
rules_of_thumb.value: Long options: collect vega, pay theta

pitfalls: Buying premium right before earnings → vol crush often eats directional gain.
Selling vega blindly when IV spikes — IV can keep climbing in real shocks.
Ignoring term structure — front-month vega and back-month vega behave differently.

entries.title: Rho
entries.subtitle: Δ option price per 1% change in interest rates — usually small except on long-dated options.

rules_of_thumb.label: LEAPS (>1y)
rules_of_thumb.value: Rho material — can move with rate changes

pitfalls: Ignoring rho on multi-year LEAPS — can be larger than vega.
Forgetting rho effect on synthetic positions (long stock + long put).

entries.title: Yield to Maturity
entries.subtitle: The total return on a bond if held to maturity, including coupons and price convergence.
pitfalls: Confusing YTM with current yield (coupon ÷ price) — they differ when bond is not at par.
Assuming you'll re-invest coupons at YTM — depends on future rate environment.
Ignoring call provisions — YTC (yield-to-call) matters for callable bonds.

entries.title: Duration
entries.subtitle: How sensitive a bond's price is to a 1% change in interest rates — measured in years.
pitfalls: SVB-style failure: long-duration bonds + sudden rate spike + deposit run = insolvency.
Ignoring convexity for large rate moves — duration is a linear approximation only.
Treating bond fund duration as fixed — managers shift duration over time.

entries.title: Convexity
entries.subtitle: Second-order rate sensitivity — accounts for the curvature in price/yield relationship.
pitfalls: Duration alone underestimates losses on big rate moves up; overestimates losses on big down.
Mortgages have negative convexity due to prepayment optionality.
Convexity is small for short-duration bonds; matters most for 10y+.

entries.title: Credit Spread
entries.subtitle: Yield premium of a corporate/risky bond over a comparable Treasury — compensation for default risk.
pitfalls: Spread ≈ default expectation only roughly; large component is liquidity premium.
Tight spreads near cycle ends signal complacency, not safety.
Spreads widen sharply in liquidity events (March 2020) regardless of fundamentals.

external_links.title: ICE BofA US HY Master II OAS

entries.title: Treasury Securities
entries.subtitle: US government debt: T-bills (≤1y), T-notes (2–10y), T-bonds (20–30y), TIPS (inflation-linked).
pitfalls: T-bills are 'risk-free' for default but not for inflation or reinvestment.
T-bonds carry significant duration risk — 10% NAV swings on rate shifts.
TIPS underperform in deflation periods (no negative inflation adjustment in some cases).

entries.title: High-Yield Bond (Junk Bond)
entries.subtitle: Corporate bond rated below investment grade (BB+ or lower) — higher yield, higher default risk.
pitfalls: Treating HY as 'high income' without acknowledging default risk and equity-like correlations in stress.
Buying HY for diversification — in real crises HY correlates with equities.
Ignoring covenant quality — 'cov-lite' loans give bondholders little protection.

entries.title: Market Order
entries.subtitle: Order to buy or sell immediately at the best available price.
pitfalls: Market orders in thin markets fill far from displayed mid — slippage destroys execution.
Market open / close has wide spreads — limit orders preferred.
Stop orders trigger market orders — same liquidity risk applies.

entries.title: Limit Order
entries.subtitle: Order to buy at or below / sell at or above a specified price — price guaranteed, fill is not.
pitfalls: Setting limit too far from market → no fill, miss the move.
GTC limits expire on most platforms after 30–90 days — may not realize.
Partial fills can leave odd-lot positions in low-volume names.

entries.title: Stop-Loss Order
entries.subtitle: Triggers a market order when price hits a defined level — designed to cap losses.

rules_of_thumb.label: Wide stop downside
rules_of_thumb.value: Larger losses when right thesis fails

pitfalls: Stops execute as market orders — gap risk through the stop level.
Round-number stops cluster; algos hunt them.
Mental stops fail under pressure; use hard stops if you'll honor them.

entries.title: Stop-Limit Order
entries.subtitle: Triggers a limit order at the stop price — price-protected but can fail to fill in fast moves.

rules_of_thumb.label: Use case
rules_of_thumb.value: Avoid bad fills in gappy markets

rules_of_thumb.label: Risk
rules_of_thumb.value: Order doesn't fill if price gaps through limit

pitfalls: On a hard gap, stop-limit may not fill — you stay in a falling position.
Often the 'safer' choice that paradoxically delivers the worst outcome in a crash.
Pure stop-loss is usually better for risk-discipline; stop-limit only for normal-vol days.

entries.title: Trailing Stop
entries.subtitle: Stop-loss that adjusts upward as price rises — locks in gains while letting winners run.
pitfalls: Trail too tight → stopped out on normal pullbacks.
Trail too wide → give back significant gains.
Doesn't help on hard down-gaps — same as regular stop.

entries.title: Bid-Ask Spread
entries.subtitle: Difference between the highest buyer price (bid) and lowest seller price (ask) — execution cost.
pitfalls: Buying at ask + selling at bid = round-trip cost = 1× spread; matters in active trading.
Spread widens at open, close, around news, and in low-volume hours.
Wide spreads are an under-appreciated cost in small-cap and crypto trading.

entries.title: Slippage
entries.subtitle: Difference between expected execution price and actual fill price.
pitfalls: Backtests usually under-model slippage; live results disappoint.
Stop-loss + thin market = stop hits at far worse price than expected.
Strategy that's fine at $10k size may be unprofitable at $1M due to slippage scaling.

entries.title: Liquidity
entries.subtitle: How easily an asset can be bought or sold without significantly moving the price.
pitfalls: Liquidity disappears in stress — same stock that traded $100M/day stops trading.
Single-counterparty risk in crypto/derivatives (FTX 2022).
Treating ETF liquidity as = underlying liquidity — they can decouple.

entries.title: Volume
entries.subtitle: Number of shares (or contracts) traded in a period — confirms or refutes price moves.
pitfalls: Confusing daily volume with average — single-day spikes mean little.
Volume in dark pools doesn't show up on lit-tape; total picture is incomplete.
Low-float stocks can have huge volume relative to size — beware short squeezes.

entries.title: Float
entries.subtitle: Shares actually available to trade — excludes insider holdings, government stakes, lockups.

rules_of_thumb.label: Float < 20M shares
rules_of_thumb.value: Susceptible to large moves on small flows

pitfalls: Confusing market cap with float — small float means real liquidity is much smaller.
Lockup expirations release new float → often pressures price downward.
Founder selling can dramatically increase float in months.

entries.title: R-Multiple
entries.subtitle: Trade outcome expressed as multiples of risked capital — speaks directly to expectancy.
pitfalls: Reporting raw $ P&L without normalizing by risk — hides whether you have an edge.
Cutting winners early reduces avg R below sustainable threshold.
Doubling down increases R-cost on losers; rarely improves expectancy.

entries.title: Expected Value
entries.subtitle: Probability-weighted average outcome of a decision — the right way to compare bets.

rules_of_thumb.label: EV > 0
rules_of_thumb.value: Bet has positive edge over time

pitfalls: Confusing EV with most-likely outcome — they often differ.
Ignoring fat-tail risk that makes 'EV' calculation invalid.
Acting on EV without enough trials to realize it (volatility eats you first).

entries.title: Kelly Criterion
entries.subtitle: Mathematically optimal fraction of capital to bet given known edge and odds.
pitfalls: Full Kelly produces brutal drawdowns even at the optimum — most pros use ¼ to ½ Kelly.
Estimating p and b incorrectly = catastrophic over-betting.
Assumes returns are independent — correlated trades break the math.

external_links.title: The Kelly Criterion in Blackjack & Investing

entries.title: Expectancy
entries.subtitle: Average expected dollar return per trade given win rate, average win, and average loss.
pitfalls: Reporting expectancy on too few trades — random noise dominates.
Forgetting to subtract commissions, slippage, and fees.
Strategies with infrequent huge winners need long samples to show true expectancy.

entries.title: SOFR
entries.subtitle: Secured Overnight Financing Rate — the benchmark replacing LIBOR for USD lending.
pitfalls: SOFR is overnight and nearly risk-free — LIBOR included bank credit risk; spreads differ.
Term SOFR (1m, 3m) exists but is not the same as overnight SOFR.
SOFR can spike at quarter-end when balance-sheet constraints reduce repo supply.

entries.title: Federal Funds Rate
entries.subtitle: The FOMC's target overnight interbank rate — the most influential short-term rate in the U.S.
pitfalls: The effective fed funds rate (EFFR) can drift a few bps from the target; that's normal.
Rate cuts don't guarantee economic stimulus if bank lending conditions tighten simultaneously.
Market pricing of future rates (OIS forwards) often diverges from the FOMC dot plot.

entries.title: Quantitative Easing
entries.subtitle: Central bank asset purchases that inject liquidity and suppress long-term yields.
pitfalls: QE does not cause hyperinflation automatically — reserves stay in the banking system.
Diminishing returns: each subsequent QE program had smaller market impact.
Unwinding (QT) is difficult; the 2019 repo spike showed reserves can fall too far.

entries.title: Quantitative Tightening
entries.subtitle: Central bank balance sheet reduction — the reverse of QE.
pitfalls: MBS rolls off slower than Treasuries because homeowners prepay less in rising-rate environments.
Monitoring reserve scarcity is imprecise — the safe floor is unknown until you cross it.
Markets focus more on the policy rate path than QT pace.

entries.title: TIPS
entries.subtitle: Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities — U.S. government bonds with inflation-adjusted principal.

rules_of_thumb.label: Best in
rules_of_thumb.note: Phantom income tax drag in taxable

pitfalls: Phantom income: CPI adjustment is taxable in the year it accrues even though not received in cash.
Real yields can be negative for extended periods — you're guaranteed to underperform inflation slightly.
TIPS ETFs have duration risk; they fall in price when real yields rise.

entries.title: Breakeven Inflation
entries.subtitle: Market-implied inflation expectation derived from TIPS vs nominal Treasury yield spread.

rules_of_thumb.label: Fed target breakeven
rules_of_thumb.note: PCE-CPI gap means BI runs above 2%

pitfalls: Breakeven includes an inflation risk premium (+20–40 bps) and liquidity premium (−10–20 bps) — not a pure inflation forecast.
Crisis periods cause TIPS to sell off (liquidity squeeze), making breakevens look deflationary when they're not.
PCE (the Fed's measure) runs ~20–30 bps below CPI.

entries.title: Forward Guidance
entries.subtitle: Central bank communication about future policy — shaping expectations to move long-term rates.

rules_of_thumb.label: Market vs dots
rules_of_thumb.note: Markets price cuts faster than dots in easing cycles

rules_of_thumb.label: Tapering tantrum
rules_of_thumb.note: Hint at slowing QE → 100 bps yield spike

pitfalls: Forward guidance creates market dependency — surprises to the downside cause outsized volatility.
Calendar-based guidance is less flexible than state-contingent guidance.
International spillovers: Fed guidance moves global rates and currencies.

entries.title: Neutral Rate
entries.subtitle: The theoretical interest rate that neither stimulates nor restrains economic growth (r*).
pitfalls: r* is unobservable and estimated with wide uncertainty — policymakers can be badly wrong for years.
Structural shifts (demographics, AI investment, fiscal deficits) may have permanently raised r*.
Using market OIS forwards as r* proxy conflates market expectations with structural equilibrium.

entries.title: Reverse Repo
entries.subtitle: Fed facility allowing money market funds to park cash overnight, setting a hard floor on short-term rates.

rules_of_thumb.label: Rate relationship
rules_of_thumb.note: Corridor: ON RRP rate ≤ EFFR ≤ IORB

pitfalls: Rapid ON RRP decline during QT signals reserves may be approaching scarcity.
The rate differential between ON RRP and T-bills drives money market fund positioning shifts.
ON RRP usage is a plumbing indicator — not directly a growth or inflation signal.

entries.title: Real Interest Rate
entries.subtitle: Nominal interest rate minus inflation — the actual return in purchasing-power terms.

rules_of_thumb.label: Positive real rate
rules_of_thumb.note: Restrictive for growth and risk assets

pitfalls: CPI vs PCE gives different real rate readings; the Fed uses PCE (runs ~20–30 bps below CPI).
Ex-ante real rate (uses expected inflation) differs from ex-post (uses actual inflation).
Negative real rates can persist for years without causing immediate inflation.

entries.title: Intrinsic Value (Options)
entries.subtitle: The immediate exercise value of an option — max(0, underlying minus strike for calls).
pitfalls: Intrinsic value is not option fair value — extrinsic value also exists.
Deep ITM options can have very wide bid-ask spreads despite large intrinsic value.
Early exercise is only rational when intrinsic exceeds extrinsic you'd forfeit.

entries.title: Extrinsic Value
entries.subtitle: The time and volatility premium in an option price beyond its intrinsic value.
pitfalls: Buying high-IV options means paying expensive extrinsic that may never be recovered.
Never exercise an option early when you can sell it and capture remaining extrinsic value.
IV crush kills extrinsic even if the stock moves in your direction.

entries.title: Moneyness
entries.subtitle: Whether an option's strike is above, at, or below the current underlying price.
pitfalls: ITM only means profitable if exercised immediately — can still expire worthless if stock reverses.
ATM-forward uses forward price (not spot) for precise options pricing.
Deep OTM options expire worthless at high frequency despite offering high percentage upside.

entries.title: Put-Call Parity
entries.subtitle: No-arbitrage relationship linking call price, put price, stock, and bond for European options.

rules_of_thumb.label: Synthetic long stock
rules_of_thumb.value: Long call + Short put + Lend PV(K)

pitfalls: Strictly holds for European options; American options require inequality bounds.
Dividend-paying stocks require adjusting for present value of dividends.
Apparent violations during stress are often due to wide bid-ask spreads, not true arbitrage.

entries.title: Covered Call
entries.subtitle: Selling a call option against shares you own — collecting premium in exchange for capped upside.
pitfalls: Covered calls cap your upside — a 5% premium sacrificed for a 50% upside move is a bad trade.
Assignment forces a taxable sale event in taxable accounts.
Rolling a short call higher when the stock surges does not recover all forgone upside.

entries.title: Iron Condor
entries.subtitle: Selling both an OTM put spread and an OTM call spread — profiting from range-bound markets.
pitfalls: Risk/reward is unfavorable: limited gain vs. larger loss — need high win rate.
Single gap move from earnings or macro shock can wipe out multiple months of premium.
Negative vega: rising IV causes mark-to-market losses even if the stock hasn't moved.

entries.title: Calendar Spread
entries.subtitle: Selling a near-dated option and buying a longer-dated option at the same strike — profiting from time decay differences.

rules_of_thumb.label: Max profit
rules_of_thumb.value: When underlying ≈ strike at short expiry

rules_of_thumb.label: Vega
rules_of_thumb.value: Positive — benefits from IV rise on back month

pitfalls: A large move away from the strike destroys both legs — total debit loss.
IV crush on earnings can hurt the back month more than expected.
Pin risk: exact expiry at the strike creates unintended delta exposure post-assignment.

entries.title: Volatility Skew
entries.subtitle: The pattern where OTM puts carry higher implied volatility than OTM calls in equity markets.
pitfalls: High skew (expensive puts) can persist indefinitely — not a timing signal for crashes.
Low skew (complacency) has preceded several major dislocations.
Single-stock skew is harder to interpret than index skew due to event-driven factors.

entries.title: Funding Rate
entries.subtitle: Periodic payment between perpetual futures longs and shorts that anchors contract price to spot.
pitfalls: High positive funding is a lagging indicator — price may have already peaked.
Funding rates differ between exchanges; arbitrage requires cross-exchange execution.
Perpetual contracts not available to U.S. retail investors on most platforms.

entries.title: Liquidation Price
entries.subtitle: The price at which a leveraged crypto position is forcibly closed due to insufficient margin.
pitfalls: Cascade liquidations amplify moves — liquidations beget more liquidations.
Mark price (not last price) triggers liquidation — exchange smoothing prevents manipulation.
Funding rate costs erode margin and bring liquidation closer without adverse price movement.

entries.title: Hash Rate
entries.subtitle: Total computational power securing a proof-of-work blockchain network.
pitfalls: Real-time hash rate is an estimate inferred from block frequency — noisy over short windows.
Hash rate growth doesn't scale linearly with security beyond a threshold.
Miner capitulation (selling BTC reserves) historically marks cycle bottoms.

entries.title: Gas Fee
entries.subtitle: Transaction cost paid to validators on Ethereum for computational work.
pitfalls: Gas fees are paid in ETH even when transacting in other tokens — you must hold ETH.
Failed transactions still consume gas fees for the computation attempted.
L2 fees are not fully independent of Ethereum mainnet gas (calldata costs).

entries.title: Staking Yield
entries.subtitle: Annualized return earned by locking proof-of-stake tokens to validate the network.
pitfalls: High yields in obscure protocols are funded by token inflation — not real economic activity.
Slashing risk: Ethereum validators can lose up to full 32 ETH stake for misbehavior.
Liquid staking concentration (Lido ~30%+ of staked ETH) creates systemic risk.

entries.title: MVRV Ratio
entries.subtitle: Market value to realized value — a Bitcoin cycle valuation gauge using on-chain cost basis.
pitfalls: MVRV at historical sell levels can stay elevated for months in a strong bull run.
Works best for Bitcoin; less reliable for altcoins with different supply dynamics.
Declining MVRV during price appreciation signals distribution — old holders selling to new buyers.

entries.title: NAV Premium / Discount
entries.subtitle: The difference between an ETF's market price and the per-share value of its underlying holdings.
pitfalls: International ETF end-of-day NAV is calculated at local close — may be 10+ hours stale.
For bond ETFs, the ETF market price can actually be more accurate than the stale NAV.
Paying a premium means you're overpaying for assets at that moment.

entries.title: Authorized Participant
entries.subtitle: Large institution with exclusive rights to create and redeem ETF shares — the arbitrage mechanism keeping ETF prices near NAV.
pitfalls: AP mechanism slows in illiquid underlying markets — bond and international ETFs most affected.
During March 2020, bond ETF APs slowed, causing ETFs to trade at significant discounts.
AP concentration risk: few APs dominate some ETFs; their withdrawal can disrupt NAV convergence.

entries.title: Tracking Error
entries.subtitle: Divergence between an ETF's returns and its benchmark index — a measure of replication quality.

rules_of_thumb.label: TE vs tracking diff
rules_of_thumb.note: TE = volatility of gap; TD = total gap

pitfalls: Low tracking error with large tracking difference: consistently underperforms by a stable amount.
Securities lending income can make tracking difference better than expense ratio alone.
Synthetic ETFs (swaps) have low TE in normal markets but add counterparty risk.

entries.title: Expense Ratio
entries.subtitle: Annual percentage of fund assets charged as management and operating fees.
pitfalls: Low expense ratio doesn't equal good tracking — verify tracking difference separately.
Net expense ratio (post-waiver) may jump to gross ratio when waivers expire.
Expense ratio excludes trading costs, bid-ask spread, and sales loads.

entries.title: NBBO
entries.subtitle: National Best Bid and Offer — the best available buy and sell prices across all U.S. exchanges.

rules_of_thumb.label: Regulation NMS
rules_of_thumb.value: Brokers must fill at NBBO or better

pitfalls: NBBO may not exist by the time your market order arrives — quotes change in microseconds.
NBBO reflects only displayed liquidity; dark pools and iceberg orders are hidden.
For thinly traded stocks, NBBO spread can be 5–10%+ of share price.

entries.title: Market Maker
entries.subtitle: Firm that continuously quotes both sides of a market, earning the spread while providing liquidity.
pitfalls: Market makers can withdraw quotes during extreme volatility — markets become one-sided.
PFOF payments from retail brokers to market makers create potential conflicts of interest.
Options market makers hedge with stock — large options flow can move the underlying.

entries.title: Payment for Order Flow
entries.subtitle: Broker practice of routing retail orders to market makers in exchange for payment.

rules_of_thumb.label: US broker revenue
rules_of_thumb.value: Hundreds of millions/year per large broker

pitfalls: Zero-commission trading funded by PFOF is not free — costs are in execution quality.
For large orders, information leakage to market makers can exceed price improvement received.
PFOF creates incentive to maximize trading frequency (gamification) for more PFOF revenue.

entries.title: Dark Pool
entries.subtitle: Private trading venue where institutional orders execute anonymously without pre-trade transparency.
pitfalls: Toxic flow (HFT in dark pools) can convert safe dark trading into information-loss scenarios.
Not all midpoint executions are genuine price improvements — some delay discovery instead.
Multiple regulatory fines for dark pool operators misleading clients about protections.

entries.title: Short Interest
entries.subtitle: Total shares sold short but not yet covered — a measure of bearish conviction and squeeze potential.
pitfalls: High short interest alone is not a buy signal — stocks with high short interest often deserve it.
Short interest above 100% of float is possible via re-hypothecation.
Synthetic short positions (puts, swaps) don't appear in standard short interest data.

entries.title: Days to Cover
entries.subtitle: How many days of average volume it would take all short sellers to buy back their positions.

rules_of_thumb.label: Low DTC
rules_of_thumb.note: Shorts can exit quickly; lower squeeze risk

pitfalls: DTC uses average volume; during a squeeze, volume spikes 10–50× making actual cover time much shorter.
High DTC alone is not a buy signal — need a catalyst to force covering.
DTC calculated on bi-weekly delayed data; current short interest can differ significantly.

entries.title: WACC
entries.subtitle: Weighted Average Cost of Capital — the blended rate a company must earn to create value.
pitfalls: Use market value weights (E and D), not book value — they're very different for growth companies.
Beta is backward-looking; future risk may differ from historical sensitivity.
Equity risk premium (ERP) estimates range 4–6%; a 1% difference shifts WACC by ~0.7%.

entries.title: DCF
entries.subtitle: Discounted Cash Flow — valuing a business by discounting its projected free cash flows to present value.

rules_of_thumb.label: Terminal value % of EV
rules_of_thumb.note: Most value is in the terminal

pitfalls: Terminal value drives 60–80% of value but is the most uncertain assumption.
Garbage in, garbage out — DCF is only as good as the underlying business assumptions.
Works poorly for early-stage or cyclical companies with negative or volatile cash flows.

entries.title: Terminal Value
entries.subtitle: Estimated value of a business at the end of the forecast period — represents all cash flows in perpetuity.

rules_of_thumb.label: Terminal growth rate
rules_of_thumb.note: Should not exceed long-run GDP growth

pitfalls: Terminal growth rate above WACC creates mathematical impossibility — always check.
Exit multiples embed current market biases — depressed multiples in recessions create artificially low TV.
Terminal year must be normalized (maintenance capex ≈ D&A, zero working capital changes).

entries.title: LBO
entries.subtitle: Leveraged Buyout — acquiring a company using substantial debt, with the target's assets as collateral.
pitfalls: High leverage amplifies downside as much as upside — 20% EBITDA decline can wipe equity.
Covenant violations can trigger lender acceleration before cash flow insolvency.
Dividend recapitalizations increase leverage post-acquisition, adding risk for remaining debtholders.

entries.title: Net Working Capital
entries.subtitle: Current assets minus current liabilities — operational liquidity and its effect on free cash flow.
pitfalls: NWC includes non-cash items — positive NWC doesn't mean you have that cash.
M&A NWC peg disputes are common — understand the target before closing.
Aggressive payables extension or receivables acceleration distorts NWC ahead of quarter-end.

entries.title: Buyback Yield
entries.subtitle: Annual share repurchase spend as a percentage of market cap — part of total shareholder yield.
pitfalls: Buybacks financed by debt add leverage risk — the yield is not truly free.
Gross buyback yield overstates return if large SBC (stock-based comp) issuance offsets it.
Companies often buy most aggressively near market tops — capital allocation quality varies.

entries.title: FCF Yield
entries.subtitle: Free cash flow divided by market cap — the cash earnings yield an investor receives.
aliases: price to free cash flow yield

rules_of_thumb.label: SBC adjustment
rules_of_thumb.value: Subtract SBC from FCF for true economic yield

pitfalls: FCF is lumpy — use 3-year average for stable yield calculation.
SBC not deducted from GAAP operating cash flow — inflates reported FCF for tech companies.
Very low FCF yield may reflect growth investment, not permanent overvaluation.

entries.title: Tail Risk
entries.subtitle: The probability and impact of extreme, low-frequency events far beyond normal distribution predictions.
pitfalls: VaR models explicitly ignore what happens in the tail — they define, not measure, tail risk.
Over-hedging via expensive OTM puts creates persistent drag exceeding the benefit in most regimes.
Historical simulation assumes the future resembles the past — it often doesn't in crises.

entries.title: CVaR
entries.subtitle: Conditional Value at Risk — the average loss in worst-case scenarios beyond the VaR threshold.

rules_of_thumb.label: CVaR vs VaR
rules_of_thumb.value: CVaR always ≥ VaR at same confidence level

rules_of_thumb.label: Basel III shift
rules_of_thumb.value: From VaR to ES (97.5%) for internal models

rules_of_thumb.label: CVaR 95%
rules_of_thumb.value: Average of worst 5% of daily returns

pitfalls: CVaR is still model-dependent — if distribution assumptions are wrong, CVaR estimates are wrong.
Historical simulation CVaR relies on past tail events being representative of future tails.
Crisis correlations spike, making portfolio CVaR much worse than sum-of-parts CVaR suggests.

entries.title: Sortino Ratio
entries.subtitle: Risk-adjusted return using only downside volatility — penalizing harmful risk, not upside moves.
pitfalls: Requires sufficient data to estimate downside deviation reliably.
A low Sortino ratio doesn't mean absolute safety — measures relative to return, not absolute loss.
As with Sharpe, past Sortino ratios don't guarantee future performance.

entries.title: Information Ratio
entries.subtitle: Active return per unit of tracking error — the primary metric for evaluating active fund management.
pitfalls: Minimum 3–5 years of data needed for statistical significance.
Strong past IR doesn't guarantee future IR — manager skill can decay.
A single big concentrated bet that worked can inflate IR meaninglessly.

entries.title: Ulcer Index
entries.subtitle: Measures depth and duration of portfolio drawdowns — the 'stress' of holding through losses.
pitfalls: Ulcer Index depends heavily on measurement period; one severe bear market dominates.
Rising markets artificially suppress UI even for risky strategies not yet tested.
Less widely reported than max drawdown — must compute from raw data.

entries.title: PEG Ratio
entries.subtitle: P/E ratio divided by expected earnings growth rate — a growth-adjusted valuation shorthand.
pitfalls: Growth rate estimates are highly uncertain — analyst 3-5yr growth estimates are notoriously inaccurate.
PEG breaks down for negative EPS or negative growth.
Quality of growth matters: debt-funded growth deserves lower multiple than organic.

entries.title: Price-to-Sales
entries.subtitle: Market cap divided by revenue — the go-to valuation for unprofitable growth companies.

rules_of_thumb.label: Rule of 40 check
rules_of_thumb.value: Revenue growth% + margin% > 40% justifies high P/S

pitfalls: P/S ignores profitability entirely — a company burning cash at 10× P/S is burning its market cap.
EV/Revenue is strictly better for cross-company comparison (adjusts for debt).
Comparing P/S across sectors is meaningless without margin context.

entries.title: Enterprise Value
entries.subtitle: Total cost to acquire a business — market cap plus net debt — capital structure neutral.

calculator.formula: Market Cap + Total Debt + Preferred + Minority Interest − Cash

rules_of_thumb.label: Negative EV
rules_of_thumb.value: Cash > market cap + debt — deep value signal

pitfalls: Stranded cash (overseas, restricted) isn't truly accessible — may not fully offset debt.
Minority interest is often forgotten; omitting it understates EV.
Operating leases should be capitalized in EV post-IFRS 16.

entries.title: Earnings Yield
entries.subtitle: EPS divided by share price — the inverse of P/E, comparable to bond yields.
pitfalls: Trailing earnings yield at cycle peaks overstates the true yield on normalized earnings.
Negative earnings make the yield meaningless.
The Fed Model (earnings yield vs bonds) assumes inflation affects both assets equally — it doesn't.

entries.title: Price-to-FCF
entries.subtitle: Market cap divided by free cash flow — a cash-based alternative to P/E.
pitfalls: FCF is lumpy — use 3-year average to avoid capex spike distortion.
GAAP FCF doesn't deduct SBC; true economic P/FCF is higher for high-SBC companies.
Maintenance vs. growth capex distinction matters — high growth capex depresses FCF legitimately.

entries.title: EV/Revenue
entries.subtitle: Enterprise value divided by revenue — capital-structure-neutral price-to-sales for growth companies.
pitfalls: Revenue quality (recurring vs. one-time) matters hugely — same ratio, very different durability.
High EV/Revenue doesn't tell you when the company will generate meaningful cash flow.
Comparing EV/Revenue across sectors is meaningless without margin expectations.

entries.title: Coupon Rate
entries.subtitle: The annual interest percentage a bond pays on its face value — fixed at issuance.
pitfalls: Coupon rate is NOT yield — a high coupon bond trading at premium may have lower YTM than expected.
For callable bonds, the relevant yield is yield-to-call, not YTM.
Zero coupon bonds have no reinvestment risk but maximum duration risk.

entries.title: Par Value
entries.subtitle: The face value of a bond — the principal repaid at maturity and the base for coupon calculation.
pitfalls: Par value is not fair value — bonds regularly trade significantly above and below par.
For callable bonds, call price is typically above par (call premium).
Inflation erodes the real value of par repayment over the bond's life.

entries.title: Yield Spread
entries.subtitle: The difference in yield between a bond and a benchmark Treasury — the price of credit risk.
pitfalls: Spread can widen from liquidity concerns even when credit quality is unchanged.
Z-spread and OAS are more rigorous than simple spread to Treasury — use them for callable bonds.
Single-name spreads can blow out from company-specific events unrelated to market conditions.

entries.title: Investment Grade
entries.subtitle: Credit rating of BBB-/Baa3 or above — the threshold for institutional bond mandates.

rules_of_thumb.label: Fallen angel
rules_of_thumb.value: IG downgraded to HY → forced selling

pitfalls: Ratings lag fundamental deterioration — Enron and Lehman were IG-rated days before failure.
BBB is the largest IG component and most vulnerable to recession downgrades (BBB cliff).
IG does not mean zero default risk — it means relatively low probability.

entries.title: Spread to Treasury
entries.subtitle: A bond's yield minus the matching-maturity Treasury yield — isolating pure credit and liquidity premium.

rules_of_thumb.label: Carry trade
rules_of_thumb.value: Spread is the carry above risk-free rate

pitfalls: Spread conflates credit risk and liquidity premium — can widen for liquidity reasons alone.
OAS is more appropriate than simple spread to Treasury for callable or MBS bonds.
TIPS-based spread provides inflation-adjusted comparison — different from nominal spread.

entries.title: Order Book
entries.subtitle: Real-time ledger of all outstanding buy and sell limit orders — the mechanism of price discovery.

rules_of_thumb.label: Deep book
rules_of_thumb.value: Low price impact per unit of order flow

rules_of_thumb.label: Level 1 vs Level 2
rules_of_thumb.value: L1: best bid/ask; L2: full depth

pitfalls: Visible order book is only displayed liquidity — dark pools and icebergs hide the full picture.
Quote stuffing (fake orders canceled in microseconds) can simulate false liquidity.
Thin books mean large orders cause significant slippage regardless of headline spreads.

entries.title: Momentum
entries.subtitle: The tendency of recent winners to keep winning (and losers to keep losing) over intermediate horizons.
pitfalls: High turnover makes momentum tax-inefficient in taxable accounts.
Momentum crashes happen without warning and can eliminate years of gains in weeks.
Transaction costs compete away the premium in less liquid markets.

entries.title: Mean Reversion
entries.subtitle: The tendency of extreme price, spread, or ratio values to return toward their long-run average.

rules_of_thumb.label: Half-life
rules_of_thumb.value: Speed of mean reversion; shorter = faster reversion

pitfalls: Mean reversion strategies fail catastrophically when the 'mean' itself shifts permanently.
Averaging down in individual stocks assuming mean reversion can lead to large losses.
Mean reversion in individual stocks is much weaker than in spreads and aggregate data.

entries.title: Position Sizing
entries.subtitle: Determining how much capital to allocate to a trade based on risk tolerance and stop distance.
pitfalls: Oversizing is the most common cause of account blowup.
Position sizing in dollar terms without considering price level leads to inconsistent risk.
Stop-loss slippage in volatile instruments makes calculated risk estimates optimistic.

entries.title: Risk/Reward Ratio
entries.subtitle: Potential loss versus potential gain on a trade — establishes a minimum standard for trade quality.

rules_of_thumb.label: At 1:3
rules_of_thumb.value: Need only 25% win rate to break even

pitfalls: R:R is only as good as the stop and target placements — arbitrary numbers are meaningless.
A 1:5 R:R trade with 5% win rate is a losing system — R:R must pair with realistic win rate.
Most trades are not binary; partial profits and trailing stops complicate simple R:R math.

entries.title: Average True Range
entries.subtitle: Volatility indicator measuring average daily price range including gaps — used for stop placement and sizing.
pitfalls: ATR is backward-looking — doesn't predict future volatility.
ATR gives magnitude not direction — a high ATR stock can be trending or whipsawing.
Short-period ATR (< 5 bars) is very noisy; 14+ periods recommended.

entries.title: Margin
entries.subtitle: Borrowed funds used to control a larger position — amplifying both gains and losses.
pitfalls: Margin amplifies losses exactly as much as gains — a 50% leveraged position loses 100% equity on a 33% move.
Interest costs compound silently — a marginally profitable trade becomes a loss over months.
Brokers can increase margin requirements without notice, triggering unexpected calls.

entries.title: Short Selling
entries.subtitle: Borrowing and selling shares you don't own, profiting if the price falls — with unlimited loss potential.
pitfalls: The market can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent — timing matters enormously.
Short sellers owe dividends paid during the short period.
Short gains are always short-term capital gains regardless of holding period.

entries.title: GDP
entries.subtitle: Gross Domestic Product — total value of goods and services produced in a country, the primary economic size measure.

rules_of_thumb.label: Technical recession
rules_of_thumb.value: 2 consecutive quarters of negative real GDP

pitfalls: GDP is released with ~30-day lag and revised twice — initial estimates can be significantly wrong.
Debt-financed government spending inflates GDP temporarily without genuine wealth creation.
GDP growth alone doesn't indicate distribution of income or sustainability.

entries.title: Inflation / CPI
entries.subtitle: Consumer Price Index — monthly measure of average price change for a basket of consumer goods and services.

rules_of_thumb.label: Shelter lag
rules_of_thumb.note: CPI shelter lags actual market rents

pitfalls: CPI-U may not reflect your personal inflation basket — housing, healthcare, and commuting vary widely.
Shelter inflation in CPI lags actual market rents by 12–18 months — over/understates turn direction.
Seasonally adjusted vs unadjusted CPI are different; financial media sometimes mixes them.

entries.title: Carry Trade
entries.subtitle: Borrowing in a low-rate currency and investing in a high-rate currency — earning the interest rate differential.

rules_of_thumb.label: Carry crash risk
rules_of_thumb.value: Funding currency appreciation wipes carry income

pitfalls: Carry trades are highly correlated with risk sentiment — unwind simultaneously in crises.
EM carry adds political risk, convertibility risk, and liquidity risk on top of rate differential.
Leverage amplifies disasters proportionally — carry with leverage is 'nickels vs steamroller.'

entries.title: Dollar-Cost Averaging
entries.subtitle: Investing a fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of price — removing market timing from the equation.

rules_of_thumb.label: DCA vs lump sum
rules_of_thumb.value: Lump sum wins ~2/3 of time in trending bull markets

pitfalls: DCA does NOT guarantee a profit or protect against permanent loss in a declining asset.
In secular bull markets, DCA costs returns vs. immediate lump sum.
DCA into a single declining stock provides no diversification — only timing diversification.

entries.title: Rebalancing
entries.subtitle: Realigning portfolio allocations back to target — selling winners and buying laggards systematically.

rules_of_thumb.label: Annual rebalancing
rules_of_thumb.value: Nearly as effective as more frequent; lower costs

rules_of_thumb.label: Rebalancing bonus
rules_of_thumb.value: Real in range-bound markets; costs in trends

pitfalls: Rebalancing in taxable accounts triggers capital gains — use tax-advantaged accounts first.
Rigid rebalancing to a wrong target allocation is worse than having the right allocation.
Rebalancing across highly correlated assets provides minimal diversification benefit.

entries.title: Sector Rotation
entries.subtitle: Shifting portfolio allocations between stock market sectors in anticipation of business cycle changes.
pitfalls: Sectors often lead the cycle — by the time rotation is obvious, most of the move has occurred.
Business cycles don't follow scripts — 2020 recession lasted only 2 months.
Frequent sector rotation generates tax events and transaction costs that compete away alpha.

entries.title: Ex-Dividend Date
entries.subtitle: The cutoff date after which new buyers don't receive the upcoming dividend.
pitfalls: Dividend capture (buy before ex-date, sell after) is generally not profitable — price drops offset dividend.
Confusing ex-date with record date or payment date — only ex-date matters for receiving dividend.
Short sellers owe dividends to the share lender on ex-dates.

entries.title: Revenue
entries.subtitle: Total sales from primary business activities — the top line of the income statement.

rules_of_thumb.label: Revenue recognition
rules_of_thumb.value: Earned over time, not when cash received (ASC 606)

rules_of_thumb.label: Red flag
rules_of_thumb.value: Revenue growing faster than cash collections

pitfalls: Revenue is the most easily manipulated metric via channel stuffing and aggressive recognition.
Identical revenue can represent very different economics depending on margin and capital requirements.
Revenue growth alone without a profitability roadmap is only valuable if unit economics support it.

entries.title: Net Income
entries.subtitle: Bottom-line profit after all expenses, interest, and taxes — the basis for EPS.

rules_of_thumb.label: Income statement order
rules_of_thumb.value: Revenue → Gross Profit → EBIT → EBT → Net Income

pitfalls: Net income is the most manipulated line — accruals, estimates, and one-time charges all affect it.
Declining share count from buybacks can grow EPS even when absolute net income is flat.
Recurring 'one-time' charges signal quality issues — verify the consistency over time.

entries.title: Debt-to-Equity
entries.subtitle: Total debt divided by shareholders' equity — a measure of financial leverage and solvency risk.
pitfalls: Use market value equity (not book value) for meaningful comparison.
D/E without interest coverage is incomplete — high D/E with strong FCF can be manageable.
Off-balance-sheet liabilities (operating leases, pensions) inflate true leverage beyond D/E.

entries.title: Leverage
entries.subtitle: Using borrowed capital to amplify returns — and risks — beyond what equity alone would allow.

rules_of_thumb.label: 2× ETF long-term
rules_of_thumb.value: Does NOT return 2× — volatility drag erodes it

pitfalls: A 50% loss requires a 100% gain to recover; leverage accelerates this problem.
Daily-reset leveraged ETFs experience volatility drag — unsuitable for buy-and-hold.
Margin calls force delevering at the worst possible time (market bottoms).

entries.title: Margin Call
entries.subtitle: Broker demand to deposit more funds when account equity falls below the maintenance margin threshold.
pitfalls: Brokers can increase requirements without notice — especially during volatility.
Meeting a margin call by selling other positions extends pain in a downturn.
Crypto exchanges often auto-liquidate instantly with no grace period.

entries.title: Circuit Breaker
entries.subtitle: Automatic trading halt triggered by extreme price declines — a market-wide or individual stock pause.
pitfalls: Circuit breakers can create front-running as investors rush to exit before a halt.
China's circuit breaker (2016) was withdrawn after it paradoxically accelerated selling.
Options strategies can become dangerous when the underlying halts — hedges become unexecutable.

entries.title: Benchmark
entries.subtitle: A standard index used to evaluate investment performance — the baseline all returns are measured against.
pitfalls: Using an inappropriate benchmark makes performance look spectacular or terrible based on cycle, not skill.
Survivorship bias in peer benchmarks: failed funds leave the peer group, inflating historical comparisons.
Changing benchmarks retroactively to make performance look better is a significant red flag.

entries.title: Smart Beta
entries.subtitle: Rules-based factor investing strategies that tilt away from market-cap weights to capture risk premia.
pitfalls: Factor premia can be crowded out — more money chasing a factor narrows future expected returns.
Factor zoo problem: hundreds of claimed factors, many from data mining.
Value factor had multi-year underperformance (2010–2020) testing even committed investors.

entries.title: Correlation
entries.subtitle: Statistical measure of how closely two assets move together, ranging from −1 to +1.
pitfalls: Historical correlation is not stable — it shifts with market regime, especially post-2022 equity/bond.
Correlation measures linear relationships; tail co-movement requires copula analysis.
Diversification across 'uncorrelated' strategies often fails in liquidity crises.
